title: Tailscale Identity Header Spoofing via Loopback Forgery
description: When gateway.auth.allowTailscale is enabled, the gateway authenticates incoming WebSocket connections based on HTTP headers (tailscale-user-login, x-forwarded-for, x-forwarded-proto, x-forwarded-host) from requests whose TCP source address is a loopback address (127.x/::1). The isTailscaleProxyRequest check at auth.ts line 97-100 only verifies isLoopbackAddress and hasTailscaleProxyHeaders; it does not cryptographically confirm that the loopback connection originated from the Tailscale daemon. Any local process that can open a TCP connection to localhost:18789 can craft these headers and impersonate any Tailscale identity.
recommendation: Do not grant authentication solely on loopback source + header presence. Verify Tailscale identity via the local Tailscale daemon's API (e.g., /localapi/v0/whois) rather than trusting injectable headers. Alternatively, bind to the Tailscale interface address exclusively when allowTailscale is true to prevent non-Tailscale loopback connections.
evidence: auth.ts lines 88-100: isTailscaleProxyRequest returns true when isLoopbackAddress(socket.remoteAddress) AND all three forwarding headers are present. Lines 142-152: if auth.allowTailscale && !localDirect, a request passing isTailscaleProxyRequest with a tailscale-user-login header receives ok:true with method:tailscale and the supplied login as the user, with no further cryptographic check.

title: Non-Timing-Safe Token Comparison in Token Auth Mode
description: In auth.mode='token', the gateway compares the supplied token against the configured secret using JavaScript strict inequality (connectAuth.token !== auth.token) at auth.ts line 175. This is not a constant-time comparison. An attacker with network access can perform a timing side-channel attack by sending many token candidates and measuring response latency to enumerate the token byte-by-byte. The password mode correctly uses timingSafeEqual via safeEqual(), but the token branch does not. The safeEqual helper is defined and available at line 31-34 but is never called for token comparisons.
recommendation: Replace connectAuth.token !== auth.token with !safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token). The safeEqual helper (lines 31-34) already wraps timingSafeEqual correctly but is only used on the password branch (line 189). Apply it identically to the token branch.
evidence: auth.ts line 31-34 defines safeEqual using timingSafeEqual. Line 189 correctly calls safeEqual(password, auth.password) for mode=password. Line 175 uses !== for mode=token, breaking constant-time guarantees. No rate-limiting is present on WebSocket connect attempts.

title: Non-Timing-Safe Hook Token Comparison Enables Timing Oracle Attack
description: The webhook endpoint token check at server-http.ts line 73 uses JavaScript strict inequality (token !== hooksConfig.token). The hook endpoint is an inbound HTTP integration point often exposed to external systems. An attacker who knows the hook base path can mount a timing-based token enumeration attack. Unlike the WebSocket path, HTTP requests are stateless and trivially parallelizable, making high-throughput timing attacks straightforward.
recommendation: Replace token !== hooksConfig.token with a constant-time comparison using Node.js crypto.timingSafeEqual. Also add per-IP rate limiting on the hooks endpoint (e.g., via a token-bucket filter) to reduce the practical window for timing attacks.
evidence: server-http.ts lines 72-78: extractHookToken returns the bearer/header/query token, and the subsequent !== comparison is a short-circuit string equality check, not constant time. The gateway defines safeEqual in auth.ts but does not import or use it in server-http.ts. No rate limiting middleware wraps the hook handler.

title: Any Authenticated WebSocket Client Can Overwrite Full Gateway Config Including Auth Secrets
description: The config.set, config.patch, and config.apply methods are exposed to every authenticated WebSocket client with no role separation. An attacker who obtains the single gateway auth token can call config.patch to modify any security-critical field: the auth token itself, exec-approval allowlists, tools.elevated.allowFrom wildcards, channel bot tokens, and AI provider API keys. After modifying the config, config.apply triggers a gateway restart that loads the new config. There is no write-only vs read-only role separation.
recommendation: Introduce a read-only vs read-write client distinction. Require a second secret (or re-authentication) for config mutations. At minimum, restrict config.set/patch/apply to loopback-origin connections even when remote access is enabled, since remote clients should only need to read status.
evidence: server-methods/config.ts lines 143-370: config.set (line 143), config.patch (line 195), and config.apply (line 273) all accept params from any authenticated client context (GatewayRequestHandlers) without any additional role check. The only guard is baseHash optimistic locking (requireConfigBaseHash), which prevents concurrent overwrites but does not restrict which authenticated clients can write. server-bridge-methods-config.ts mirrors this pattern for the bridge channel (lines 130-265).

title: Elevated Tool Execution Wildcard '*' Grants Any Messaging-Channel Sender Host Shell Access
description: The isApprovedElevatedSender function in reply-elevated.ts explicitly short-circuits to true when any entry in the allowFrom list is '*'. This means setting tools.elevated.allowFrom to include '*' grants every sender on every configured messaging channel (Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, Signal, iMessage) the ability to trigger arbitrary shell command execution on the host. The security audit flags this combination as critical, but the gateway continues to operate with no runtime guard preventing this configuration from being active.
recommendation: Reject the '*' wildcard at config validation time so that a configuration containing allowFrom: ['*'] fails writeConfigFile. If wildcard is needed for testing, require a separate --allow-elevated-wildcard startup flag. Add a runtime guard in isApprovedElevatedSender that logs a critical warning and returns false when wildcard is detected in production mode.
evidence: reply-elevated.ts line 62-64: any allowFrom entry equal to '*' causes the function to return true unconditionally, bypassing all sender identity checks. resolveElevatedPermissions (line 101) calls isApprovedElevatedSender with the config-supplied allowFrom without any additional wildcard guard before the call.

title: OpenAI-Compatible Endpoint Allows Cross-Session Injection via Attacker-Controlled 'user' Field
description: The /v1/chat/completions endpoint builds a persistent session key from the OpenAI 'user' field supplied in the request body (openai-http.ts line 199: mainKey = user ? 'openai-user:${user}' : ...). Any authenticated caller can set user to an arbitrary string, causing their request to be appended to a pre-existing session belonging to another caller who used the same user value. This allows session history poisoning across callers who share the same gateway token (e.g., a team using the gateway as a shared LLM proxy).
recommendation: Namespace session keys with the authenticated caller's identity (e.g., hash the bearer token and include it in the session key prefix). Validate that x-clawdbot-session-key headers, if present, were issued to the requesting caller. Document the isolation model in the API reference.
evidence: openai-http.ts lines 189-201 (resolveSessionKey): the function accepts an arbitrary string from payload.user (line 248: const user = typeof payload.user === 'string' ? payload.user : undefined) and constructs the full session key from it without tying it to the authenticated identity. Two callers using the same 'user' value will share the same agent session history.

title: No Rate Limiting on WebSocket Connections or Agent Invocations
description: The gateway imposes no per-IP, per-connection, or global rate limits on WebSocket connection attempts, authentication retries, or agent command invocations. The agent handler (server-methods/agent.ts) dispatches agentCommand calls that trigger LLM API calls and potentially shell command execution. An authenticated attacker can flood the gateway with concurrent agent requests to exhaust API provider quotas, exhaust memory from LLM response buffers, or degrade service for legitimate users. Lane-based concurrency (applyGatewayLaneConcurrency) only serializes per-session, not globally.
code_snippet:
void agentCommand(
  { message, images, ... },
  defaultRuntime,
  context.deps,
)
.then(...)
.catch(...);
recommendation: Add configurable per-connection and global concurrency limits for agent invocations. Implement connection-rate limiting at the WebSocket upgrade handler. Expose clawdbot.json5 options for max-concurrent-runs-per-session and max-concurrent-runs-global. Consider circuit-breaker logic to reject new runs when API-error rates are elevated.
evidence: server-methods/agent.ts line 301: agentCommand is dispatched with void (fire-and-forget). No concurrent-run counter or semaphore is checked before dispatching. The dedupe cache (context.dedupe) only prevents exact-idempotencyKey duplicates; different idempotency keys spawn independent runs. server-bridge-methods-chat.ts chat.send follows the same pattern (line 390: void agentCommand(...)).

title: Raw Error Strings Returned in HTTP 500 Responses Expose Internal Details
description: The HTTP server catch handler at server-http.ts line 236 calls res.end(String(err)), which converts the Node.js Error object to its string representation. This may include the exception message, which can reveal internal file system paths, module names, database error messages, or other infrastructure details. The OpenAI-compatible endpoint at openai-http.ts line 441 similarly writes Error: ${String(err)} directly into an SSE event streamed to the caller.
recommendation: Replace String(err) in HTTP responses with a generic 'Internal server error' message. Log the full error detail (including stack trace) server-side using the existing subsystem logger. Optionally include a correlation ID in the response for support lookups.
evidence: server-http.ts lines 233-237: the async IIFE's .catch handler directly writes String(err) to the response body. openai-http.ts lines 431-445: the catch block for the streaming agent command writes 'Error: ' + String(err) into an SSE delta event. Both paths expose raw exception messages to any caller who can trigger an internal error.

title: Docker Sandbox Defaults Off: Agent Commands Execute Directly on Host OS
description: The Docker sandbox is opt-in and disabled by default. When not enabled, all bash-tools, web-tools, and browser automation calls execute directly in the host Node.js process under the user's OS account with no filesystem restriction, no process isolation, and no network isolation. An authorized messaging-channel user or a prompt-injected LLM output can trigger arbitrary command execution on the host. The exec-approval allowlist is the only defense, but it cannot anticipate all LLM-generated command variations and relies on accurate pre-approval.
recommendation: Make the Docker sandbox the default for new installations. Show a prominent startup warning when sandbox is disabled and bash tools or elevated tools are enabled. Add a configuration field that must be explicitly set to false to acknowledge operating without a sandbox, preventing accidental unsandboxed deployments.
evidence: bash-tools.exec.ts: the sandbox config (BashSandboxConfig) is passed as a parameter and is not required to be non-null before spawning processes. sandbox/config.ts resolveSandboxDockerConfig returns null when docker sandbox is not configured. agent-scope.ts and pi-embedded.ts wire the sandbox config from the clawdbot.json5 agents.sandbox section, which defaults to absent (unsandboxed).

title: LAN Bind with auth.mode='none' Allows Unauthenticated Access to Any LAN Device
description: When gateway.bind is set to 'lan' or 'auto' (which selects LAN when Tailscale is unavailable) and gateway.auth.mode is 'none', the gateway listens on 0.0.0.0 and accepts all connections from any device on the local network without credentials. The security audit flags this combination as critical but the gateway starts and remains fully operational. Any device on the same WiFi or LAN can connect, read conversation history, modify config, and issue agent commands that execute bash on the host.
recommendation: Block gateway startup (do not just warn) when the bind address is non-loopback and auth.mode is 'none'. Require an explicit --insecure-no-auth CLI flag to override. Alternatively, auto-generate a random token during first start and require it for all non-loopback binds. The audit advisory should not be the sole enforcement mechanism.
evidence: auth.ts lines 164-166: when auth.mode is 'none', authorizeGatewayConnect returns ok:true with no further checks. server.impl.ts lines 85-93 document that bind='lan' maps to 0.0.0.0. resolveGatewayRuntimeConfig selects bind based on config without blocking on no-auth+non-loopback combinations. The security audit emits a critical finding but does not call process.exit or throw.

title: Cron Agent Jobs Execute Without Channel-Level Allowlist Enforcement
description: Cron jobs configured in clawdbot.json5 are dispatched as isolated agent runs by server-cron.ts / CronService. These runs invoke agentCommand directly without passing through the channel-level allowFrom, dmPolicy, or groupPolicy gating that protects inbound message processing from messaging channels. An attacker who obtains the gateway auth token can inject new cron jobs via config.patch that execute on schedule, bypassing all channel-level authorization. The injected jobs persist until the cron entry is manually removed.
recommendation: Require elevated authorization for config.patch mutations that add or modify cron entries. Log cron job creation, modification, and execution with full attribution. Validate cron job commands against the exec-approval allowlist before scheduling. Consider requiring explicit cron job signing or a separate cron-management credential.
evidence: server-cron.ts lines 66-77: runCronIsolatedAgentTurn is called directly from the CronService without any channel allowFrom check. The session key is 'cron:{jobId}' which does not correspond to any messaging-channel identity. server-methods/config.ts config.patch (line 195) accepts any authenticated WebSocket client's patch, including cron job additions, with no additional authorization.

title: Extension Plugins Register HTTP Handlers and Tool Providers with Full Host Access
description: Extension plugins loaded by loadGatewayPlugins and loadClawdbotPlugins receive full access to the gateway runtime context, including the broadcast bus, config read/write, and the Node.js file system. Plugins can register HTTP route handlers that bypass gateway authentication, hook agent events to exfiltrate transcripts, or read ~/.clawdbot/ secrets. There is no permission sandbox, no capability declaration requirement, and no code signing for plugins. The only existing control is that workspace plugins are part of the monorepo, but community plugins installed via npm would receive identical unrestricted access.
recommendation: Implement an explicit plugin capability model requiring declared permissions (e.g., 'needs: [http, config-read, tool-exec]') with user approval during installation. Run third-party plugins in a separate Node.js worker_thread or child process with a limited IPC interface. Add plugin integrity verification (hash or signature) checked at load time.
evidence: server.impl.ts lines 180-186: loadGatewayPlugins is called with the full cfg, workspaceDir, log, and gateway handler registry. Plugins returned by pluginRegistry receive the full gateway context. server-bridge-methods-config.ts handleConfigBridgeMethods passes writeConfigFile and readConfigFileSnapshot directly to any bridge client that invokes config methods.

title: Tailscale Funnel Exposes Gateway to Unrestricted Public Internet Without Rate Limiting
description: When gateway.tailscale.mode is 'funnel', enableTailscaleFunnel is called and the gateway becomes reachable from the public internet. There is no rate limiting on WebSocket connection attempts, HTTP hook requests, or authentication retries once funnel is active. Internet scanners can discover the endpoint within minutes. Unauthenticated attackers can mount token brute-force attempts, timing oracle attacks on the hook token, and connection-flood DoS attacks. The security audit reports this as critical but does not prevent funnel from activating.
recommendation: Block gateway startup when tailscale.mode='funnel' and auth.mode='none'. Require explicit startup confirmation before enabling funnel. Implement connection-rate limiting and IP-based blocking at the application layer when funnel is active. Integrate an optional reverse-proxy (nginx/caddy) layer for funnel deployments with built-in rate limiting.
evidence: server-tailscale.ts lines 21-25: enableTailscaleFunnel is called unconditionally when tailscaleMode === 'funnel'. No rate-limiter middleware is applied anywhere in the HTTP or WebSocket stack. The audit finding is emitted by src/security/audit.ts but does not gate startup. server-http.ts and the WS upgrade handler have no connection-rate-limiting code.

title: WhatsApp Session Credentials Stored in Plaintext Under ~/.clawdbot/sessions/
description: The WhatsApp channel uses the Baileys library, which stores long-term identity keys, pre-keys, and session state in plaintext files under ~/.clawdbot/sessions/. These files are functionally equivalent to a full WhatsApp account credential and cannot be revoked without account re-registration. Any same-user process (e.g., malware, a compromised npm package, another agent tool) can read and exfiltrate these files to allow full impersonation of the victim's WhatsApp account on a different machine.
code_snippet:
// WhatsApp session state stored via Baileys at ~/.clawdbot/sessions/ by the web channel monitor
// No encryption at rest; same-user readable files
recommendation: Encrypt WhatsApp session files at rest using a machine-specific key derived from the OS keychain (macOS Keychain Services, Linux Secret Service). Enforce 0600 file permissions on all files under ~/.clawdbot/sessions/. Alert users on startup if session directory permissions are too permissive. Consider periodic session re-authentication to limit the validity window of stolen credentials.
evidence: CLAUDE.md documents sessions stored under ~/.clawdbot/sessions/. The Baileys library stores its auth state in the directory specified by the channel configuration; no encryption wrapper is applied. The security audit checks permissions but does not enforce encryption. THREAT-019 in the threat model confirms the same finding.

title: AI Provider API Keys Stored in Plaintext in ~/.clawdbot/auth-profiles.json
description: All AI provider API keys (Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq, Gemini, Deepgram, MiniMax, etc.) and OAuth tokens are stored as plaintext JSON in ~/.clawdbot/auth-profiles.json and ~/.clawdbot/credentials/. Any co-resident process running under the same OS user account can read and exfiltrate these credentials without any additional authentication or decryption step.
code_snippet:
// API keys written to ~/.clawdbot/auth-profiles.json as plaintext JSON
// File permissions advisory only via security audit
recommendation: Encrypt API keys at rest using the OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service, or a key derived from a master password). Restrict file permissions to 0600 at write time (not just as an advisory audit finding). Consider using environment variables or OS-native secret storage as the primary credential store.
evidence: CLAUDE.md: 'Web provider stores creds at ~/.clawdbot/credentials/'. Security audit checks file permissions but does not enforce encryption. The auth-profiles.json file is written by auth-choice flows without any encryption wrapper. Same-user processes can trivially cat ~/.clawdbot/auth-profiles.json to obtain all API keys.

title: Agent Session Transcripts Stored in Plaintext JSONL Files
description: All agent conversation history (messages, tool call results, command outputs, file contents, web page content) is stored in plaintext JSONL files under ~/.clawdbot/agents/<agentId>/sessions/*.jsonl. If the home directory is synced to a cloud service (iCloud, Dropbox, OneDrive) or if file permissions are too permissive, these transcripts are exposed. Transcripts may contain sensitive business data, credentials mentioned in conversation, and command outputs.
recommendation: Encrypt session transcripts at rest. Add warnings during setup about cloud sync risks. Enforce 0600 permissions on all files under ~/.clawdbot/ at write time. Consider storing transcripts outside the default home directory sync path (e.g., /var/lib/clawdbot/ or a user-specified encrypted volume).
evidence: server-bridge-methods-chat.ts line 92: appendFileSync writes plaintext JSON to the transcript path. The transcriptPath is derived from storePath in the ~/.clawdbot/agents/ directory tree. No encryption is applied before or after the write. The security audit checks for synced folder locations but does not block operation.

title: Security-Sensitive Operations Lack Structured Audit Logging
description: The gateway does not produce persistent, structured audit logs for: authentication success/failure events (with source IP and method), config.set/patch/apply mutations (before/after diff), elevated tool activation events, webhook agent dispatch (with caller IP), or session transcript reads (chat.history). An insider or attacker who modifies config, escalates privilege, or exfiltrates session data can deny their actions without a forensic log trail.
recommendation: Emit structured audit log entries to an append-only file in ~/.clawdbot/ for: (1) authentication success/failure with IP and method, (2) all config mutations with before/after diff, (3) elevated tool activation events, (4) webhook agent dispatch with caller IP, (5) chat.history and session read operations. Consider HMAC-signing audit entries to detect tampering.
evidence: auth.ts lines 134-196: authorizeGatewayConnect returns GatewayAuthResult but writes nothing to a persistent audit log. server-methods/config.ts config.patch (line 195) calls writeConfigFile without logging a diff. server-bridge-methods-chat.ts chat.history (line 116) reads and returns session messages with no audit trail.

title: Swabble VoiceWake Hook Executes Arbitrary Config-Supplied Command With Speech Text as Argument
description: The Swabble voice wake daemon (HookExecutor.swift) spawns a process using config.hook.command with config.hook.args plus the transcribed speech text (payload) as a final positional argument. The speech text is derived from live microphone input via on-device speech recognition and is not sanitized before being passed to the subprocess. If the transcribed text contains shell metacharacters and the hook command is a shell interpreter, or if an attacker can influence the wake-word recognition output (adversarial audio), they could inject commands. Additionally, the config file at ~/.config/swabble/config.json is written without encryption or access-control enforcement.
recommendation: Pass the speech text only via environment variables (SWABBLE_TEXT is already set), not as a positional argument. Validate and sanitize the transcribed text before use as an argument if direct argument passing is required. Document that config.hook.command must be a binary, not a shell, to prevent shell injection. Restrict config.json to 0600 permissions.
evidence: HookExecutor.swift lines 38-43: the payload string (prefix + job.text, where job.text is the raw transcribed speech) is appended to process.arguments. If config.hook.command points to /bin/sh and config.hook.args contains ['-c'], any adversarially transcribed text becomes a shell command. The SWABBLE_TEXT env var (line 46) already provides a safe injection-resistant alternative.
